text: The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to remove the omentum, revealing areas of gangrene and visible fluid accumulation.
Using two atraumatic graspers, the surgeon performs detorsion of the ovary, visibly rotating it back to its normal position. This maneuver is repeated three times to ensure proper alignment.

The surgeon completes the correction of the ovarian torsion. A hemorrhagic cyst is punctured using a harmonic device, and the inner cystic layer is separated. Two Maryland dissectors are used to hold the inner cystic layer while the outer layer is being separated. A thorough lavage will be performed to remove the hemorrhagic content.

The cystic content is placed in the anterior cul-de-sac. The surgeon uses a stone-holding forceps or an endo bag to remove the friable cyst, which has impaired blood supply due to torsion. Proper irrigation and suction are performed to clear the area.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate adhesions, preparing to remove the cyst. Following this, thorough lavage is performed to cleanse the surgical area.

The surgeon performs plication of the elongated ovarian ligament using 2-0 Prolene suture in a purse-string manner. A grasper provides lateral traction on the ovary while a needle holder is used to take several consecutive bites over the ligament in this three-port laparoscopic surgery.

The surgeon tightens an extracorporeal square knot on the ovarian ligament using proline suture, ensuring plication of the ligament. Multiple knots are tied to prevent slipping, followed by cutting the excess suture.
The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the surgical area, ensuring all debris and fluid are cleared before concluding the procedure.
